title: Validation leakage in microbiome machine learning
text: Microbiome machine-learning models often work samples, scientific unit generalization may subject, hospital, sequencing run, visit, study site. train test folds split units incorrectly, model can learn repeated-subject structure batch signatures instead biology. vignette focuses three common leakage patterns: repeated measurements subject, outcome labels aligned batch center, time-ordered samples subjects. MOAT train machine-learning model. audits metadata modeling recommends validation schemes make leakage harder miss.
code: library(moat) data("toy_moat")

title: Validation leakage in microbiome machine learning
text: Row-wise cross-validation treats sample independent. assumption fragile several rows come person. small simulated dataset , every subject contributes two samples. ordinary row-wise folds used, one sample subject can training fold another sample subject test fold. test fold longer independent subject-level evaluation. actionable recommendation keep samples subject together inside resampling split.
code: repeated_biome <- toy_moat SummarizedExperiment::colData(repeated_biome)$subject <- rep(   paste0("S", seq_len(ncol(repeated_biome) / 2)),   each = 2 ) SummarizedExperiment::colData(repeated_biome)$timepoint <- rep(   c("baseline", "followup"),   times = ncol(repeated_biome) / 2 ) metadata <- as.data.frame(SummarizedExperiment::colData(repeated_biome))  head(metadata) #>     sample_id outcome   batch subject timepoint #> S01       S01 Control Batch_1      S1  baseline #> S02       S02 Control Batch_1      S1  followup #> S03       S03 Control Batch_1      S2  baseline #> S04       S04 Disease Batch_1      S2  followup #> S05       S05 Control Batch_1      S3  baseline #> S06       S06 Disease Batch_1      S3  followup repeated <- check_repeated_measures(metadata, subject = "subject")  repeated[c(   "n_samples",   "n_subjects",   "n_repeated_subjects",   "max_samples_per_subject",   "risk",   "recommended_cv" )] #> $n_samples #> [1] 40 #>  #> $n_subjects #> [1] 20 #>  #> $n_repeated_subjects #> [1] 20 #>  #> $max_samples_per_subject #> [1] 2 #>  #> $risk #> [1] "high" #>  #> $recommended_cv #> [1] "grouped_cv_by_subject" head(repeated$samples_per_subject) #>   subject n_samples repeated #> 1      S1         2     TRUE #> 2     S10         2     TRUE #> 3     S11         2     TRUE #> 4     S12         2     TRUE #> 5     S13         2     TRUE #> 6     S14         2     TRUE

title: Validation leakage in microbiome machine learning
text: check_leakage() combines repeated-measure, batch, temporal diagnostics. repeated measures without explicit time variable, recommended scheme grouped cross-validation subject. downstream modeling, means fold assignment happen subject level first. rows subject move together train test.
code: leakage_grouped <- check_leakage(   metadata,   outcome = "outcome",   subject = "subject" )  leakage_grouped$recommended_cv #> [1] "grouped_cv_by_subject" leakage_grouped$recommendations #> [1] "Overall leakage risk is high."                                                  #> [2] "Multiple samples share subject IDs; use grouped cross-validation by subject."   #> [3] "No batch variable provided; batch-driven validation leakage was not evaluated." #> [4] "No time variable provided; temporal leakage was not evaluated."

title: Validation leakage in microbiome machine learning
text: Another failure mode appears center, site, sequencing batch aligned outcome. model can perform well recognizing center-specific microbiome technical signatures. batch level contains one outcome group. random row-wise split can put centers train test, making center recognition look like outcome prediction. scenario, leave-one-batch-strategy useful stress test. asks whether model still works whole center batch held .
code: confounded_metadata <- data.frame(   outcome = rep(c("Control", "Disease"), each = 10),   batch = rep(c("Center_A", "Center_B"), each = 10) )  table(confounded_metadata$batch, confounded_metadata$outcome) #>            #>            Control Disease #>   Center_A      10       0 #>   Center_B       0      10 batch_leakage <- check_leakage(   confounded_metadata,   outcome = "outcome",   batch = "batch" )  batch_leakage$risk #> [1] "high" batch_leakage$recommended_cv #> [1] "leave_one_batch_out_cv" batch_leakage$batch_leakage$summary #>   batch  n n_batch_levels n_outcome_levels empty_cells #> 1 batch 20              2                2           2 #>   batch_levels_single_outcome positivity_score effect_size effect_size_name #> 1                           2              0.5           1        cramers_v #>   risk #> 1 high

title: Validation leakage in microbiome machine learning
text: Longitudinal data add another constraint: train folds silently use future samples subject predict earlier held-samples. repeated subjects span timepoints, MOAT recommends grouped time-aware strategy. replace full temporal validation design. flags ordinary row-wise cross-validation enough metadata structure supplied.
code: leakage_time <- check_leakage(   metadata,   outcome = "outcome",   subject = "subject",   time = "timepoint" )  leakage_time$risk #> [1] "high" leakage_time$recommended_cv #> [1] "grouped_time_aware_cv_by_subject" leakage_time$temporal_leakage[c(   "n_timepoints",   "subjects_with_multiple_timepoints",   "risk" )] #> $n_timepoints #> [1] 2 #>  #> $subjects_with_multiple_timepoints #> [1] 20 #>  #> $risk #> [1] "high"

title: Validation leakage in microbiome machine learning
text: leakage recommendations flow check_biome() plan_analysis(). lets validation choice sit next design, batch, correction diagnostics instead handled afterthought.
code: audit <- check_biome(   repeated_biome,   outcome = "outcome",   subject = "subject",   time = "timepoint",   distances = "bray",   n_perm = 99,   verbose = FALSE )  summary(audit) #>  #> ── MOAT audit ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Overall risk: HIGH #>  #> ── Main warnings ── #>  #> • Overall leakage risk is high. #> • Multiple samples share subject IDs; use grouped cross-validation by subject. #> • No batch variable provided; batch-driven validation leakage was not #> evaluated. #> • Repeated subjects span multiple timepoint values; use grouped time-aware #> validation by subject. #>  #> ── Recommended next steps ── #>  #> • Overall leakage risk is high. #> • Multiple samples share subject IDs; use grouped cross-validation by subject. #> • No batch variable provided; batch-driven validation leakage was not #> evaluated. #> • Repeated subjects span multiple timepoint values; use grouped time-aware #> validation by subject. plan <- plan_analysis(audit)  plan$ml_validation #> $scheme #> [1] "grouped_time_aware_cv_by_subject" #>  #> $reason #> [1] "Use grouped time-aware validation because repeated subjects span multiple timepoints." plan$permutation #> $scheme #> [1] "restricted_by_subject_and_time" #>  #> $strata #> [1] "subject" #>  #> $reason #> [1] "Repeated subjects span multiple timepoints; preserve subject grouping and temporal order."

title: Validation leakage in microbiome machine learning
text: Validation leakage fold assignment. operation learns full dataset splitting can leak information test folds. fitting model, check following steps estimated inside training fold applied held-fold: feature filtering, prevalence filtering, normalization scaling parameters, imputation, feature selection, hyperparameter tuning.
code: audit$leakage$preprocessing_checklist #> [1] "Fit transformations, feature filtering, scaling, imputation, and feature selection inside each training fold." #> [2] "Do not use test-fold labels, batches, subjects, or timepoints when estimating preprocessing parameters."       #> [3] "Report the validation split variable used for grouped, batch-aware, or time-aware resampling."

title: Validation leakage in microbiome machine learning
text: Use checklist interpreting microbiome ML performance: multiple samples subject kept fold? outcome aligned center batch, leave-one-center-leave-one-batch-performance reported? longitudinal data, validation respect subject grouping time? preprocessing, filtering, scaling, feature selection nested inside resampling? ordinary CV leakage-aware CV results shown side side risk moderate high?

title: Auditing microbiome study designs with MOAT
text: Microbiome analyses sensitive study design. Outcome groups can confounded center, sequencing run, extraction kit, visit, subject identity. happens, downstream analyses can report biological differences partly entirely driven technical design structure. MOAT audits risks analysis. automatically correct counts decide final model. Instead, returns diagnostics, warnings, plots, analysis-plan recommendations help make study design explicit. crossing microbiome analysis, check moat.

title: Auditing microbiome study designs with MOAT
text: example dataset small simulated SummarizedExperiment count data sample metadata.
code: library(moat) data("toy_moat")  toy_moat #> class: SummarizedExperiment  #> dim: 50 40  #> metadata(0): #> assays(1): counts #> rownames(50): Taxon_001 Taxon_002 ... Taxon_049 Taxon_050 #> rowData names(0): #> colnames(40): S01 S02 ... S39 S40 #> colData names(3): sample_id outcome batch head(SummarizedExperiment::colData(toy_moat)) #> DataFrame with 6 rows and 3 columns #>       sample_id     outcome       batch #>     <character> <character> <character> #> S01         S01     Control     Batch_1 #> S02         S02     Control     Batch_1 #> S03         S03     Control     Batch_1 #> S04         S04     Disease     Batch_1 #> S05         S05     Control     Batch_1 #> S06         S06     Disease     Batch_1

title: Auditing microbiome study designs with MOAT
text: examples vignettes use small number permutations. Real analyses generally use larger value, default 999.
code: audit <- check_biome(   toy_moat,   outcome = "outcome",   batch = "batch",   distances = "bray",   n_perm = 99,   verbose = FALSE )  summary(audit) #>  #> ── MOAT audit ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Overall risk: HIGH #>  #> ── Main warnings ── #>  #> • Batch audit for bray distance has high risk (batch R2 = 0.948; PERMANOVA = #> high, dispersion = low, PCoA = high). #>  #> ── Recommended next steps ── #>  #> • Batch signal is strong relative to outcome or ordination structure; report #> batch diagnostics before downstream analysis. #> • Avoid interpreting outcome effects without sensitivity analyses that account #> for batch. #> • Batch adjustment appears statistically identifiable based on metadata #> diagnostics. #> • Overall leakage risk is low. #> • No subject variable provided; repeated-measure leakage was not evaluated. #> • Batch variables appear balanced enough for standard validation. #> • No time variable provided; temporal leakage was not evaluated.

title: Auditing microbiome study designs with MOAT
text: design module checks whether metadata variables associated outcome. categorical variables stores contingency table, Cramer’s V, empty-cell counts, conservative risk label. plot_design() shows outcome distribution across audited categorical variable. Empty cells outlined can indicate confounding complete separation.
code: audit$design[, c(   "variable",   "role",   "variable_type",   "effect_size_name",   "effect_size",   "empty_cells",   "risk" )] #>   variable  role variable_type effect_size_name effect_size empty_cells risk #> 1    batch batch   categorical        cramers_v  0.05057217           0  low plot_design(audit, variable = "batch")

title: Auditing microbiome study designs with MOAT
text: Batch adjustment meaningful outcome batch perfectly aliased. correction module summarizes whether ordinary adjustment appears identifiable metadata. diagnostic command correct data. warning kinds downstream adjustment statistically defensible.
code: audit$correction$feasibility #> [1] "safe" audit$correction$recommendations #> [1] "Batch adjustment appears statistically identifiable based on metadata diagnostics."

title: Auditing microbiome study designs with MOAT
text: batch module combines distance-based PERMANOVA, dispersion diagnostics, ordination-axis association checks. summary table reports much microbiome variation attributed outcome batch. plot_variance() visualizes PERMANOVA R2 terms. batch term exceeds outcome term strong signal downstream analysis needs explicit batch sensitivity checks.
code: audit$batch$summary[, c(   "distance",   "outcome_r2",   "batch_r2",   "batch_dominance_score",   "permanova_risk",   "permdisp_risk",   "pcoa_risk",   "risk" )] #>   distance  outcome_r2  batch_r2 batch_dominance_score permanova_risk #> 1     bray 0.003494571 0.9477919              271.2184           high #>   permdisp_risk pcoa_risk risk #> 1           low      high high plot_variance(audit, distance = "bray")

title: Auditing microbiome study designs with MOAT
text: Validation leakage can occur samples subject, batch, time structure split across train test folds way makes prediction look better . MOAT reports recommended cross-validation scheme based metadata supplied check_biome().
code: audit$leakage$recommended_cv #> [1] "standard_cv" audit$leakage$recommendations #> [1] "Overall leakage risk is low."                                              #> [2] "No subject variable provided; repeated-measure leakage was not evaluated." #> [3] "Batch variables appear balanced enough for standard validation."           #> [4] "No time variable provided; temporal leakage was not evaluated."

title: Auditing microbiome study designs with MOAT
text: plan_analysis() translates audit recommended formulas, validation schemes, batch strategy, sensitivity analyses. plan read structured starting point. Analysts still need choose methods appropriate scientific question, data distribution, experimental design.
code: plan_analysis(audit) #>  #> ── MOAT analysis plan ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Overall risk: HIGH #>  #> ── Recommended formulas ── #>  #> • Differential abundance: `~ outcome + batch` #> • PERMANOVA: `distance ~ outcome + batch` #>  #> ── Validation ── #>  #> • standard_cv: Standard cross-validation is acceptable for the supplied leakage #> variables. #>  #> ── Batch strategy ── #>  #> • sensitivity_required: Batch explains substantial microbiome variation; report #> analyses with explicit batch sensitivity checks. #>  #> ── Sensitivity analyses ── #>  #> • Repeat microbiome association analyses with and without batch terms where #> identifiable. #> • Report distance-specific PERMANOVA results and batch R2 alongside outcome R2. #>  #> ── Warnings ── #>  #> ! Batch audit for bray distance has high risk (batch R2 = 0.948; PERMANOVA = high, dispersion = low, PCoA = high). #> ! Batch-dominated microbiome signal requires explicit sensitivity analysis.

title: MOAT: Microbiome Omics Audit Toolkit
text: MOAT Microbiome Omics Audit Toolkit. crossing microbiome analysis, check moat. moat audits microbiome omics study designs downstream statistical machine-learning analysis. checks whether outcome, batch, covariates, repeated measures, correction identifiability, microbiome distance structure create risks reported differential abundance, PERMANOVA, ordination, predictive modeling. package study-audit layer. silently correct counts, remove samples, choose final model. Instead, returns structured diagnostics, risk levels, warnings, plots, analysis-plan suggestions analysts can review report.

title: MOAT: Microbiome Omics Audit Toolkit
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("xec-cm/moat")

title: MOAT: Microbiome Omics Audit Toolkit
text: audit object keeps module-level diagnostics: plan_analysis() translates audit downstream analysis guidance:
code: library(moat)  data("toy_moat")  audit <- check_biome(     toy_moat,     outcome = "outcome",     batch = "batch",     distances = "bray",     n_perm = 99,     verbose = FALSE )  summary(audit) #>  #> ── MOAT audit ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Overall risk: HIGH #>  #> ── Main warnings ── #>  #> • Batch audit for bray distance has high risk (batch R2 = 0.948; PERMANOVA = #> high, dispersion = low, PCoA = high). #>  #> ── Recommended next steps ── #>  #> • Batch signal is strong relative to outcome or ordination structure; report #> batch diagnostics before downstream analysis. #> • Avoid interpreting outcome effects without sensitivity analyses that account #> for batch. #> • Batch adjustment appears statistically identifiable based on metadata #> diagnostics. #> • Overall leakage risk is low. #> • No subject variable provided; repeated-measure leakage was not evaluated. #> • Batch variables appear balanced enough for standard validation. #> • No time variable provided; temporal leakage was not evaluated. audit$design[, c("variable", "role", "effect_size_name", "effect_size", "risk")] #>   variable  role effect_size_name effect_size risk #> 1    batch batch        cramers_v  0.05057217  low audit$batch$summary[, c("distance", "outcome_r2", "batch_r2", "risk")] #>   distance  outcome_r2  batch_r2 risk #> 1     bray 0.003494571 0.9477919 high audit$correction$feasibility #> [1] "safe" audit$leakage$recommended_cv #> [1] "standard_cv" plan_analysis(audit) #>  #> ── MOAT analysis plan ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Overall risk: HIGH #>  #> ── Recommended formulas ── #>  #> • Differential abundance: `~ outcome + batch` #> • PERMANOVA: `distance ~ outcome + batch` #>  #> ── Validation ── #>  #> • standard_cv: Standard cross-validation is acceptable for the supplied leakage #> variables. #>  #> ── Batch strategy ── #>  #> • sensitivity_required: Batch explains substantial microbiome variation; report #> analyses with explicit batch sensitivity checks. #>  #> ── Sensitivity analyses ── #>  #> • Repeat microbiome association analyses with and without batch terms where #> identifiable. #> • Report distance-specific PERMANOVA results and batch R2 alongside outcome R2. #>  #> ── Warnings ── #>  #> ! Batch audit for bray distance has high risk (batch R2 = 0.948; PERMANOVA = high, dispersion = low, PCoA = high). #> ! Batch-dominated microbiome signal requires explicit sensitivity analysis.

title: MOAT: Microbiome Omics Audit Toolkit
text: plot_design() visualizes outcome distribution across audited categorical variable. Empty cells highlighted often signal confounding complete separation. plot_variance() visualizes PERMANOVA R2 term, making batch dominance easy spot.
code: plot_design(audit, variable = "batch") plot_variance(audit, distance = "bray")

title: MOAT: Microbiome Omics Audit Toolkit
text: Design confounding: whether outcome associated batch covariates metadata. Correction feasibility: whether batch adjustment statistically identifiable observed design. Microbiome batch effects: whether batch explains microbiome variation using PERMANOVA, dispersion, ordination diagnostics. Validation leakage: whether repeated measures, batch-outcome association, time structure require grouped time-aware validation. Analysis planning: recommended formulas, permutation restrictions, validation schemes, batch strategy, sensitivity analyses.

title: MOAT: Microbiome Omics Audit Toolkit
text: moat intentionally conservative. high-risk audit prove analysis invalid, low-risk audit prove downstream models safe. package designed make design limitations explicit conclusions interpreted right sensitivity analyses reporting context.

what: Check microbiome batch effects across distances — check_batch
title: Check microbiome batch effects across distances — check_batch
text: check_batch() combines distance calculation, PERMANOVA, dispersion, PCoA diagnostics batch-risk summary. Full dispersion diagnostics stored dispersion; permdisp remains batch-compatibility table. PCoA diagnostics include coordinates, variance explained, axis--metadata association tests.

title: Check microbiome batch effects across distances — check_batch
code: check_batch(   x,   metadata = NULL,   outcome,   batch = NULL,   covariates = NULL,   assay = "counts",   distances = c("aitchison", "bray"),   n_perm = 999 )

title: Check microbiome batch effects across distances — check_batch
text: x numeric matrix-like object SummarizedExperiment::SummarizedExperiment() object. metadata Optional data frame sample metadata. x SummarizedExperiment, colData(x) used default. outcome single string naming outcome variable metadata. batch Optional character vector naming batch variables metadata. covariates Optional character vector naming covariates metadata. assay single string naming assay extract x SummarizedExperiment. Defaults "counts". distances character vector naming microbiome distances. Supported values accepted compute_biome_distance(). n_perm single positive integer giving number permutations.

title: Check microbiome batch effects across distances — check_batch
text: list batch audit diagnostics recommendations.

title: Check microbiome batch effects across distances — check_batch

what: Audit a microbiome study design — check_biome
title: Audit a microbiome study design — check_biome
text: check_biome() main entry point MOAT. validates SummarizedExperiment-like object, records audit parameters, returns stable moat_audit object. Design correction diagnostics evaluated metadata; batch diagnostics combine distance-based PERMANOVA, PERMDISP, PCoA audits. Leakage diagnostics evaluate repeated measures, batch-driven validation leakage, optional temporal leakage.

title: Audit a microbiome study design — check_biome
code: check_biome(   x,   outcome,   batch = NULL,   covariates = NULL,   subject = NULL,   time = NULL,   assay = "counts",   transform = "clr",   distances = c("aitchison", "bray"),   n_perm = 999,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Audit a microbiome study design — check_biome
text: x SummarizedExperiment::SummarizedExperiment() object. Objects extending SummarizedExperiment, TreeSummarizedExperiment, accepted. outcome single string naming outcome variable colData(x). batch Optional character vector naming batch variables colData(x). covariates Optional character vector naming covariates colData(x). subject Optional single string naming subject identifier variable colData(x). time Optional single string naming time variable colData(x). assay single string naming assay audit. Defaults "counts". transform single string naming microbiome transformation record downstream audit modules. Defaults "clr". distances character vector naming microbiome distances record downstream audit modules. Defaults c("aitchison", "bray"). n_perm single positive integer planned number permutations downstream audit modules. Defaults 999. verbose single logical value indicating whether future audit modules report progress. Defaults TRUE.

title: Audit a microbiome study design — check_biome
text: moat_audit object.

title: Audit a microbiome study design — check_biome
code: data("toy_moat")  audit <- check_biome(   toy_moat,   outcome = "outcome",   batch = "batch" )  is_biome_audit(audit) #> [1] TRUE

title: Check batch correction feasibility — check_correction
text: list correction feasibility diagnostics, including balance diagnostics, model matrix diagnostics, positivity score, feasibility category, recommendation text.

title: Check batch correction feasibility — check_correction
code: metadata <- data.frame(   condition = rep(c("control", "case"), each = 6),   center = rep(c("A", "B"), times = 6),   age = seq(30, 41) )  check_correction(metadata, outcome = "condition", batch = "center", covariates = "age") #> $status #> [1] "evaluated" #>  #> $module #> [1] "correction" #>  #> $feasibility #> [1] "caution" #>  #> $positivity_score #> [1] 1 #>  #> $balance #>    batch  n n_batch_levels n_outcome_levels empty_cells min_cell_count #> 1 center 12              2                2           0              3 #>   batch_levels_single_outcome positivity_score     risk     counts  proportions #> 1                           0                1 moderate 3, 3, 3, 3 0.5, 0.5.... #>  #> $model_matrix #>                     formula  n n_parameters rank rank_deficient #> 1 ~condition + center + age 12            4    4          FALSE #>   condition_number risk aliased_columns #> 1              844  low                 #>  #> $recommendations #> [1] "Batch adjustment may be possible, but report balance diagnostics and run sensitivity analyses." #>

title: Check experimental design metadata associations with the outcome — check_design

title: Check experimental design metadata associations with the outcome — check_design
code: metadata <- data.frame(   condition = rep(c("control", "case"), each = 6),   center = rep(c("A", "B"), times = 6),   age = c(32, 35, 37, 34, 36, 38, 52, 55, 57, 54, 56, 58) )  check_design(   metadata,   outcome = "condition",   batch = "center",   covariates = "age" ) #>   variable      role variable_type  n n_variable_levels n_outcome_levels #> 1   center     batch   categorical 12                 2                2 #> 2      age covariate    continuous 12                NA                2 #>             test     p_value effect_size             effect_size_name #> 1         fisher 1.000000000    0.000000                    cramers_v #> 2 kruskal-wallis 0.003947752    9.258201 standardized_mean_difference #>   empty_cells min_cell_count complete_separation imbalance_ratio     risk #> 1           0              3               FALSE               1 moderate #> 2          NA             NA                  NA               1     high #>   contingency_table  group_means group_medians #> 1        3, 3, 3, 3                            #> 2                   55.33333....    55.5, 35.5

what: Check multivariate dispersion differences across metadata variables — check_dispersion
title: Check multivariate dispersion differences across metadata variables — check_dispersion
text: check_dispersion() runs vegan::betadisper() permutation tests precomputed sample distance matrix. Strong dispersion differences indicate PERMANOVA effects may partly reflect spread differences rather centroid shifts.

title: Check multivariate dispersion differences across metadata variables — check_dispersion
code: check_dispersion(distance, metadata, variables, n_perm = 999)

title: Check multivariate dispersion differences across metadata variables — check_dispersion
text: distance dist object containing sample--sample distances. metadata data frame sample metadata. variables character vector naming metadata variables test. n_perm single positive integer giving number permutations.

title: Check multivariate dispersion differences across metadata variables — check_dispersion
text: data frame one row per variable.

title: Check multivariate dispersion differences across metadata variables — check_dispersion
code: data("toy_moat") metadata <- as.data.frame(SummarizedExperiment::colData(toy_moat)) distance <- compute_biome_distance(toy_moat, distance = "bray") check_dispersion(distance, metadata, variables = c("outcome", "batch"), n_perm = 99) #>   variable    status n_groups    statistic p_value risk error #> 1  outcome evaluated        2 0.0009879928    0.91  low  <NA> #> 2    batch evaluated        2 0.4410051761    0.49  low  <NA>

title: Check validation leakage risk — check_leakage
text: check_leakage() combines repeated-measure, batch-driven, temporal validation-leakage diagnostics ML workflows.

title: Check validation leakage risk — check_leakage
code: check_leakage(metadata, outcome, subject = NULL, batch = NULL, time = NULL)

title: Check validation leakage risk — check_leakage
text: metadata data frame sample metadata. outcome single string naming outcome variable metadata. subject Optional single string naming subject identifier variable. batch Optional character vector naming batch variables. time Optional single string naming time variable.

title: Check validation leakage risk — check_leakage
text: list leakage diagnostics, CV recommendations, preprocessing leakage checklist.

title: Check validation leakage risk — check_leakage
code: metadata <- data.frame(   outcome = rep(c("Control", "Disease"), each = 4),   patient_id = rep(paste0("P", 1:4), each = 2) ) check_leakage(metadata, outcome = "outcome", subject = "patient_id") #> $status #> [1] "evaluated" #>  #> $module #> [1] "leakage" #>  #> $risk #> [1] "high" #>  #> $recommended_cv #> [1] "grouped_cv_by_patient_id" #>  #> $repeated_measures #> $repeated_measures$status #> [1] "evaluated" #>  #> $repeated_measures$module #> [1] "repeated_measures" #>  #> $repeated_measures$subject #> [1] "patient_id" #>  #> $repeated_measures$n_samples #> [1] 8 #>  #> $repeated_measures$n_subjects #> [1] 4 #>  #> $repeated_measures$n_repeated_subjects #> [1] 4 #>  #> $repeated_measures$n_repeated_samples #> [1] 8 #>  #> $repeated_measures$max_samples_per_subject #> [1] 2 #>  #> $repeated_measures$samples_per_subject #>   subject n_samples repeated #> 1      P1         2     TRUE #> 2      P2         2     TRUE #> 3      P3         2     TRUE #> 4      P4         2     TRUE #>  #> $repeated_measures$risk #> [1] "high" #>  #> $repeated_measures$recommended_cv #> [1] "grouped_cv_by_patient_id" #>  #> $repeated_measures$recommendations #> [1] "Multiple samples share subject IDs; use grouped cross-validation by patient_id." #>  #>  #> $batch_leakage #> $batch_leakage$status #> [1] "skipped" #>  #> $batch_leakage$module #> [1] "batch_leakage" #>  #> $batch_leakage$risk #> [1] "unknown" #>  #> $batch_leakage$summary #> data frame with 0 columns and 0 rows #>  #> $batch_leakage$recommended_cv #> [1] "standard_cv" #>  #> $batch_leakage$recommendations #> [1] "No batch variable provided; batch-driven validation leakage was not evaluated." #>  #>  #> $temporal_leakage #> $temporal_leakage$status #> [1] "skipped" #>  #> $temporal_leakage$module #> [1] "temporal_leakage" #>  #> $temporal_leakage$time #> NULL #>  #> $temporal_leakage$subject #> NULL #>  #> $temporal_leakage$n_timepoints #> [1] NA #>  #> $temporal_leakage$subjects_with_multiple_timepoints #> [1] NA #>  #> $temporal_leakage$risk #> [1] "unknown" #>  #> $temporal_leakage$recommended_cv #> [1] "standard_cv" #>  #> $temporal_leakage$recommendations #> [1] "No time variable provided; temporal leakage was not evaluated." #>  #>  #> $preprocessing_checklist #> [1] "Fit transformations, feature filtering, scaling, imputation, and feature selection inside each training fold." #> [2] "Do not use test-fold labels, batches, subjects, or timepoints when estimating preprocessing parameters."       #> [3] "Report the validation split variable used for grouped, batch-aware, or time-aware resampling."                 #>  #> $recommendations #> [1] "Overall leakage risk is high."                                                   #> [2] "Multiple samples share subject IDs; use grouped cross-validation by patient_id." #> [3] "No batch variable provided; batch-driven validation leakage was not evaluated."  #> [4] "No time variable provided; temporal leakage was not evaluated."                  #>

what: Check whether metadata alone predicts the outcome — check_metadata_predictability
title: Check whether metadata alone predicts the outcome — check_metadata_predictability
text: check_metadata_predictability() fits simple metadata-logistic model binary outcomes evaluates stratified cross-validation. High predictability suggests design confounding validation leakage risk microbiome features used.

title: Check whether metadata alone predicts the outcome — check_metadata_predictability
code: check_metadata_predictability(   metadata,   outcome,   predictors,   n_folds = 5,   seed = 1 )

title: Check whether metadata alone predicts the outcome — check_metadata_predictability
text: metadata data frame sample metadata. outcome single string naming binary outcome variable metadata. predictors character vector naming metadata variables used predictors. n_folds single positive integer giving requested number cross-validation folds. effective number folds reduced smallest outcome class smaller n_folds. seed single integer seed used make fold assignment reproducible.

title: Check whether metadata alone predicts the outcome — check_metadata_predictability
text: list model formula, predictors used, dropped predictors, cross-validated balanced accuracy, apparent balanced accuracy, risk, warnings, recommendations.

title: Check whether metadata alone predicts the outcome — check_metadata_predictability
code: metadata <- data.frame(   outcome = rep(c("control", "case"), each = 8),   center = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 8) )  check_metadata_predictability(   metadata,   outcome = "outcome",   predictors = "center",   n_folds = 4 ) #> $status #> [1] "evaluated" #>  #> $module #> [1] "metadata_predictability" #>  #> $formula #> [1] "outcome ~ center" #>  #> $outcome #> [1] "outcome" #>  #> $outcome_levels #> [1] "case"    "control" #>  #> $predictors #> [1] "center" #>  #> $dropped_predictors #> character(0) #>  #> $n #> [1] 16 #>  #> $n_folds #> [1] 4 #>  #> $actual_folds #> [1] 4 #>  #> $metric #> [1] "balanced_accuracy" #>  #> $cv_balanced_accuracy #> [1] 1 #>  #> $apparent_balanced_accuracy #> [1] 1 #>  #> $class_counts #>   outcome n #> 1    case 8 #> 2 control 8 #>  #> $folds #>   fold n_test balanced_accuracy #> 1    1      4                 1 #> 2    2      4                 1 #> 3    3      4                 1 #> 4    4      4                 1 #>  #> $risk #> [1] "high" #>  #> $warnings #> [1] "Metadata-only model predicts outcome with high risk (CV balanced accuracy = 1.000)." #>  #> $recommendations #> [1] "Metadata alone predicts the outcome strongly; treat downstream microbiome models as high risk for design confounding." #> [2] "Use validation splits that block or stratify by the predictive metadata variables when possible."                      #>

title: Check model matrix identifiability — check_model_matrix

text: list PERMANOVA diagnostics.

title: Check PERMANOVA variation explained by metadata variables — check_permanova
code: data("toy_moat") metadata <- as.data.frame(SummarizedExperiment::colData(toy_moat)) distance <- compute_biome_distance(toy_moat, distance = "bray") check_permanova(distance, metadata, outcome = "outcome", batch = "batch", n_perm = 99) #> $status #> [1] "evaluated" #>  #> $module #> [1] "permanova" #>  #> $formula #> [1] "distance ~ outcome + batch" #>  #> $n_perm #> [1] 99 #>  #> $terms #>            term    role df sum_of_squares          r2  statistic p_value #> outcome outcome outcome  1    0.004004636 0.003494571   2.654277    0.07 #> batch     batch   batch  1    1.086130965 0.947791939 719.888915    0.01 #>  #> $outcome_r2 #> [1] 0.003494571 #>  #> $batch_r2 #> [1] 0.9477919 #>  #> $covariate_r2 #> [1] NA #>  #> $batch_dominance_score #> [1] 271.2184 #>  #> $risk #> [1] "high" #>  #> $warnings #> character(0) #>

what: Compute distances between microbiome samples — compute_biome_distance
title: Compute distances between microbiome samples — compute_biome_distance
text: compute_biome_distance() returns dist object samples. Input matrices must features rows samples columns. SummarizedExperiment input, selected assay extracted first.

title: Compute distances between microbiome samples — compute_biome_distance
code: compute_biome_distance(   x,   assay = "counts",   transform = "auto",   distance = "aitchison",   pseudocount = 1 )

title: Compute distances between microbiome samples — compute_biome_distance
text: x numeric matrix-like object SummarizedExperiment::SummarizedExperiment() object. assay single string naming assay extract x SummarizedExperiment. Defaults "counts". transform single string naming transformation apply distance calculation. Use "auto" choose "clr" Aitchison, "relative" Bray-Curtis, "presence_absence" Jaccard. distance single string naming distance. Supported values "aitchison", "bray", "jaccard". pseudocount single positive number added CLR transformation.

title: Compute distances between microbiome samples — compute_biome_distance
text: dist object samples.

title: Compute distances between microbiome samples — compute_biome_distance
code: counts <- matrix(c(0, 2, 4, 1, 3, 5), nrow = 3) colnames(counts) <- c("S1", "S2") compute_biome_distance(counts, distance = "aitchison") #>           S1 #> S2 0.3814209

what: Generate a downstream analysis plan from a MOAT audit — plan_analysis
title: Generate a downstream analysis plan from a MOAT audit — plan_analysis
text: plan_analysis() converts moat_audit object structured, readable downstream analysis plan. rerun audit statistics; summarizes existing audit diagnostics recommended formulas, validation schemes, batch strategy, sensitivity analyses.

title: Generate a downstream analysis plan from a MOAT audit — plan_analysis
code: plan_analysis(audit, verbose = FALSE)

title: Generate a downstream analysis plan from a MOAT audit — plan_analysis
text: audit moat_audit object. verbose single logical value. TRUE, include module-level risk reasons printed plan. Defaults FALSE.

title: Generate a downstream analysis plan from a MOAT audit — plan_analysis
text: moat_analysis_plan object.

title: Generate a downstream analysis plan from a MOAT audit — plan_analysis
code: data("toy_moat") audit <- check_biome(toy_moat, outcome = "outcome", batch = "batch", n_perm = 99) plan_analysis(audit) #>  #> ── MOAT analysis plan ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Overall risk: HIGH #>  #> ── Recommended formulas ── #>  #> • Differential abundance: `~ outcome + batch` #> • PERMANOVA: `distance ~ outcome + batch` #>  #> ── Validation ── #>  #> • standard_cv: Standard cross-validation is acceptable for the supplied leakage #> variables. #>  #> ── Batch strategy ── #>  #> • sensitivity_required: Batch explains substantial microbiome variation; report #> analyses with explicit batch sensitivity checks. #>  #> ── Sensitivity analyses ── #>  #> • Repeat microbiome association analyses with and without batch terms where #> identifiable. #> • Report distance-specific PERMANOVA results and batch R2 alongside outcome R2. #>  #> ── Warnings ── #>  #> ! Batch audit for aitchison distance has high risk (batch R2 = 0.945; PERMANOVA = high, dispersion = low, PCoA = high). #> ! Batch audit for bray distance has high risk (batch R2 = 0.948; PERMANOVA = high, dispersion = low, PCoA = high). #> ! Batch-dominated microbiome signal requires explicit sensitivity analysis.

text: audit moat_audit object. color Optional single string naming metadata variable. NULL, first batch variable used available, otherwise outcome. distance Optional character vector naming audited distances plot. NULL, first available distance used. Use "" plot audited distances. aspect single string controlling panel aspect. "auto" uses available plotting space easier read PC1 PC2 different ranges. "equal" preserves 1:1 coordinate ratio.

title: Plot PCoA ordination coordinates from a batch audit — plot_ordination
text: ggplot2::ggplot() object.

title: Plot PCoA ordination coordinates from a batch audit — plot_ordination
code: data("toy_moat") audit <- check_biome(toy_moat, outcome = "outcome", batch = "batch", n_perm = 99) plot_ordination(audit, color = "batch", distance = "bray")

what: Plot PERMANOVA variance explained by audited terms — plot_variance
title: Plot PERMANOVA variance explained by audited terms — plot_variance
text: plot_variance() visualizes PERMANOVA R2 terms stored batch audit. Outcome, batch, covariate, terms colored separately batch dominance easy inspect.

title: Plot PERMANOVA variance explained by audited terms — plot_variance
code: plot_variance(audit, distance = NULL)

title: Plot PERMANOVA variance explained by audited terms — plot_variance
text: audit moat_audit object. distance Optional character vector naming audited distances plot. NULL, first available distance used. Use "" plot audited distances.

title: Plot PERMANOVA variance explained by audited terms — plot_variance
text: ggplot2::ggplot() object.

title: Plot PERMANOVA variance explained by audited terms — plot_variance
code: data("toy_moat") audit <- check_biome(toy_moat, outcome = "outcome", batch = "batch", n_perm = 99) plot_variance(audit, distance = "bray")

what: Render a MOAT audit HTML report — report

text: report() renders self-contained HTML report moat_audit object. report uses package-local R Markdown template, require internet access rendering.

title: Render a MOAT audit HTML report — report
text: audit moat_audit object. file Output HTML file path. Defaults "moat_report.html". quiet single logical value passed rmarkdown::render(). Defaults TRUE. ... Additional arguments passed rmarkdown::render().

title: Render a MOAT audit HTML report — report
text: normalized output file path, invisibly.

title: Toy MOAT Microbiome Dataset — toy_moat
text: small, simulated SummarizedExperiment object examples testing. dataset contains artificial read counts 50 taxa across 40 samples, along sample metadata including outcome batch variable. generated exhibit strong batch effect first half taxa.

title: Toy MOAT Microbiome Dataset — toy_moat
code: data(toy_moat)

title: Toy MOAT Microbiome Dataset — toy_moat
text: SummarizedExperiment object 50 features (taxa) 40 samples. assays single assay named "counts" containing matrix simulated read counts (Poisson distributed). colData DataFrame containing sample metadata: sample_id: Unique sample identifier. outcome: Binary outcome variable ("Control", "Disease"). batch: Batch variable ("Batch_1", "Batch_2").

title: Toy MOAT Microbiome Dataset — toy_moat
text: Simulated data. See data-raw/simulate_toy_data.R generation script.

title: Toy MOAT Microbiome Dataset — toy_moat
code: data("toy_moat")  # Inspect the object dimensions dim(toy_moat) #> [1] 50 40  # Check sample metadata head(SummarizedExperiment::colData(toy_moat)) #> DataFrame with 6 rows and 3 columns #>       sample_id     outcome       batch #>     <character> <character> <character> #> S01         S01     Control     Batch_1 #> S02         S02     Control     Batch_1 #> S03         S03     Control     Batch_1 #> S04         S04     Disease     Batch_1 #> S05         S05     Control     Batch_1 #> S06         S06     Disease     Batch_1  # View assay counts for the first 5 taxa and 5 samples SummarizedExperiment::assay(toy_moat)[1:5, 1:5] #>           S01 S02 S03 S04 S05 #> Taxon_001  89 103 103  99  82 #> Taxon_002  96  96  89 101 109 #> Taxon_003  92  92 123 102  94 #> Taxon_004 110 103  76 112 109 #> Taxon_005  98 110 103  94 106

what: Transform a feature-by-sample microbiome matrix — transform_biome
title: Transform a feature-by-sample microbiome matrix — transform_biome
text: transform_biome() applies common microbiome transformations preserving feature sample names. Input matrices expected features rows samples columns.

title: Transform a feature-by-sample microbiome matrix — transform_biome
code: transform_biome(x, method = "clr", pseudocount = 1)

title: Transform a feature-by-sample microbiome matrix — transform_biome
text: x numeric matrix-like object features rows samples columns. method single string naming transformation. Supported values "clr", "relative", "presence_absence". pseudocount single positive number added CLR transformation.

title: Transform a feature-by-sample microbiome matrix — transform_biome
text: numeric matrix dimensions dimnames x.

title: Transform a feature-by-sample microbiome matrix — transform_biome
moat 0.99.0
text: Renamed repositioned package moat, Microbiome Omics Audit Toolkit. MOAT framed pre-analysis audit layer: crossing microbiome analysis, check moat. Added moat_audit, summary.moat_audit, moat_analysis_plan package object classes.

title: moat 0.99.0
text: Initial development release prepared future Bioconductor submission. Added moat_audit object returned check_biome(). Added input validation SummarizedExperiment-like microbiome objects. Added toy microbiome data examples, tests, vignettes.

title: moat 0.99.0
text: Added check_design() metadata associations outcome. Added check_continuous_design() continuous covariates. Added check_balance() check_model_matrix() correction feasibility diagnostics. Added check_correction() summarize whether batch adjustment statistically identifiable.

title: moat 0.99.0
text: Added transform_biome() compute_biome_distance() supported microbiome transformations distances. Added check_permanova() distance-based PERMANOVA diagnostics. Added check_batch() PERMANOVA, dispersion, PCoA-axis batch audits across selected distances.

title: moat 0.99.0
text: Added check_repeated_measures() check_leakage() subject, batch, time-aware validation risk. Added central risk scoring summary.moat_audit(). Added conservative low, moderate, high, critical, unknown risk handling.

title: moat 0.99.0
text: Added plan_analysis() downstream formula, validation, batch strategy, sensitivity recommendations. Added plot_design() visualize outcome distribution across audited categorical metadata variables. Added plot_variance() visualize PERMANOVA R2 terms batch dominance. Added report() render offline HTML reports moat_audit objects.

title: moat 0.99.0
text: Added introductory vignette covering full audit workflow. Added validation-leakage vignette microbiome machine-learning workflows. Updated README current user-facing APIs. Added citation metadata placeholder development package.
